text.title: Hybrid Search Fusion Strategies
text.summary: Rules for choosing between RRF and linear fusion, alpha parameter tuning, and score normalization requirements for combining vector and BM25 rankings.

rule.statement: Use Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) as the default fusion strategy. Use linear score normalization only after benchmarking shows improvement on the specific corpus.
rule.rationale: RRF operates on rank positions rather than raw scores, making it robust to scale differences between BM25 (unbounded, corpus-dependent) and cosine similarity (0–1). Linear fusion requires careful per-corpus normalization to avoid BM25 scores dominating.

rule.statement: Never compare raw BM25 scores across different corpora or index configurations.
rule.rationale: BM25 scores are distribution-dependent. A score of 10 on a small corpus is not comparable to a score of 10 on a large corpus. Always normalize BM25 within the same retrieval call before fusion.

rule.statement: Start alpha at 0.5 (50% semantic / 50% keyword) and tune per domain using a labeled evaluation set of at least 50 queries.
rule.rationale: There is no universally optimal alpha. Technical corpora with many exact identifiers tend toward lower alpha (more keyword weight). Prose-heavy corpora tend toward higher alpha (more semantic weight).

rule.statement: Log the alpha value used per query in production to build a calibration dataset for automated tuning.
rule.rationale: Dynamic alpha selection improves retrieval quality but requires empirical data. Without logging, there is no basis for improving the alpha selection heuristics over time.

example.note: RRF parameter k=60 is the standard value from the original paper. Adjust only with empirical justification.

text.reference: Provider-agnostic RRF fusion of ranked ID lists
